statement: Infants have proportionally larger heads and tongues compared to adults making airway obstruction more likely.
rationale: Infants have large heads relative to body size and large tongues relative to oral cavity. These anatomical differences make airway obstruction more common and airway management more challenging in pediatric patients.

statement: The pediatric airway is more anterior and superior than the adult airway.
rationale: The pediatric larynx sits higher in the neck and more forward. This affects airway positioning. The sniffing position works well for infants and children while hyperextension can actually kink the flexible pediatric airway.

statement: Infants are obligate nose breathers for the first several months of life.
rationale: Newborns and young infants breathe primarily through their nose. Nasal congestion or obstruction can cause significant respiratory distress in infants. Suctioning the nose can dramatically improve breathing.

statement: The narrowest part of the pediatric airway is at the cricoid cartilage rather than the vocal cords.
rationale: In children under 8 years the narrowest airway point is the cricoid ring below the vocal cords. In adults the narrowest point is at the vocal cords. This affects equipment sizing and is why uncuffed tubes were traditionally used in young children.

statement: Children have more flexible ribs that provide less protection to underlying organs.
rationale: Pediatric ribs are more cartilaginous and flexible. They bend rather than break absorbing energy that is transmitted to underlying organs. A child can have significant internal injury without rib fractures. Rib fractures in children indicate severe force.

statement: The Pediatric Assessment Triangle evaluates appearance work of breathing and circulation to skin.
rationale: The PAT is a rapid across-the-room assessment. Appearance includes tone interactivity consolability look and speech or cry. Work of breathing assesses sounds positioning and retractions. Circulation evaluates skin color. Abnormality indicates a sick child.

statement: Normal heart rate for an infant is 100-160 beats per minute which is higher than adult normal.
rationale: Pediatric vital signs differ significantly from adults. Infants have higher heart rates 100-160 and respiratory rates 30-60 with lower blood pressures. Using adult normal values for children will result in missed abnormalities.

statement: A blood pressure cuff that is too small will give a falsely low reading.
rationale: A cuff that is too small gives falsely HIGH readings. A cuff too large gives falsely low readings. Proper cuff size width should cover two-thirds of the upper arm is essential for accurate pediatric blood pressure measurement.

statement: Capillary refill time is a useful assessment of perfusion in children.
rationale: Capillary refill is assessed by pressing on the nail bed or forehead and measuring time for color to return. Normal is less than 2 seconds. Delayed refill suggests poor perfusion. It is more reliable in children than adults and is part of circulation assessment.

statement: Fontanelles in infants can provide information about hydration and intracranial pressure.
rationale: The anterior fontanelle normally feels flat and soft. A sunken fontanelle suggests dehydration. A bulging fontanelle can indicate increased intracranial pressure from infection or bleeding. This is a valuable assessment finding in infants.

statement: Children compensate well for illness or injury and can appear stable until sudden decompensation.
rationale: Pediatric patients have excellent compensatory mechanisms maintaining vital signs despite significant pathology. When compensation fails they crash rapidly and without warning. Do not be reassured by normal vital signs in a sick or injured child.

statement: Most pediatric cardiac arrests result from respiratory failure rather than primary cardiac problems.
rationale: Unlike adults whose arrests are usually cardiac in origin children typically arrest from respiratory causes. Hypoxia from respiratory failure leads to bradycardia then cardiac arrest. This is why airway and breathing are especially critical in pediatric resuscitation.

statement: Bradycardia in a child is often a sign of hypoxia and impending cardiac arrest.
rationale: Tachycardia is usually the first response to stress in children. Bradycardia indicates severe hypoxia or other critical illness and suggests decompensation. A bradycardic child is in imminent danger of cardiac arrest. Treat the underlying cause immediately.

statement: Fever in infants under 3 months old is always a medical emergency requiring evaluation.
rationale: Young infants have immature immune systems and can have serious bacterial infections with minimal symptoms. Fever in infants under 3 months requires urgent evaluation to rule out sepsis meningitis and UTI. Do not attribute fever to minor illness.

statement: Croup typically causes a barking cough and inspiratory stridor in young children.
rationale: Croup is viral laryngotracheobronchitis causing subglottic swelling. It produces a distinctive barking or seal-like cough with inspiratory stridor. It typically affects children 6 months to 3 years and is usually worse at night.

statement: Epiglottitis is now rare due to vaccination but when present is a life-threatening emergency.
rationale: Haemophilus influenzae type B vaccine dramatically reduced epiglottitis incidence. When it occurs it causes rapid airway obstruction. Signs include high fever drooling tripod posture and stridor. Do not examine the throat as this can trigger complete obstruction.

statement: Children who are seizing should have objects placed in their mouth to prevent tongue biting.
rationale: Never place objects in the mouth of a seizing patient of any age. This can cause injury to teeth or soft tissue and objects can be aspirated. Protect the child from injury by clearing the area but do not restrain or insert anything in the mouth.

statement: Febrile seizures in young children are usually brief and self-limited.
rationale: Febrile seizures typically occur in children 6 months to 5 years with rapid temperature rise. They are usually generalized lasting less than 15 minutes and are followed by a brief postictal period. While frightening they are usually benign.

statement: Signs of shock may be more subtle in children than in adults.
rationale: Children maintain blood pressure through intense vasoconstriction masking shock until late. Subtle signs include tachycardia mottled or pale skin delayed capillary refill weak peripheral pulses and altered mental status. Hypotension is a late and ominous sign.

statement: Minimum systolic blood pressure for children can be estimated as 70 plus twice the age in years.
rationale: The formula 70 plus 2 times age in years gives the lower limit of normal systolic BP for children 1-10 years. For example a 4-year-old should have SBP of at least 78. Below this threshold indicates hypotension requiring intervention.

statement: Palliative approach and allowing the child to remain with the parent often improves assessment.
rationale: Children are often frightened by strangers and medical equipment. Allowing the child to stay with the parent assessing from toe to head and using a calm approach reduce anxiety. A calm child is easier to assess than a frightened screaming child.

statement: Medical equipment should be hidden from view to avoid frightening pediatric patients.
rationale: Showing children equipment and letting them touch it often reduces fear. Hiding equipment then suddenly producing it can increase anxiety. Age-appropriate explanations and allowing exploration of equipment often help gain cooperation.

statement: Bruises in various stages of healing may indicate non-accidental trauma.
rationale: Bruises of different colors indicating different ages especially in patterns or protected areas may suggest abuse occurring over time. Document findings objectively including location size color and shape. Report suspected abuse per mandatory reporting laws.

statement: Injuries inconsistent with the stated mechanism or developmental ability should raise concern for abuse.
rationale: A fractured femur in a non-walking infant or burns in a splash pattern on a toddler who reportedly pulled hot water on themselves warrant suspicion. When injury does not match explanation or the child's abilities consider non-accidental trauma.

statement: EMRs are mandatory reporters of suspected child abuse in most jurisdictions.
rationale: Healthcare providers including EMRs are mandatory reporters required by law to report suspected child abuse. You do not need to prove abuse only have reasonable suspicion. Document objectively and report per your protocols and local laws.

statement: Aging causes decreased lung elasticity and chest wall compliance reducing respiratory reserve.
rationale: Age-related changes include stiffening of chest wall loss of lung elasticity and decreased respiratory muscle strength. Elderly patients have less reserve for respiratory stress and may decompensate more quickly with respiratory illness.

statement: Elderly patients have decreased ability to thermoregulate making them susceptible to temperature extremes.
rationale: Aging impairs temperature regulation through decreased sweating reduced vasoconstriction and diminished shivering. Elderly patients are at higher risk for both hypothermia and heat illness and may not recognize symptoms.

statement: Skin becomes thinner and more fragile with age increasing risk of injury and slow healing.
rationale: Aging causes thinning of the dermis and reduced collagen. Elderly skin tears and bruises easily and heals slowly. Handle elderly patients gently use care with tape and bandages and recognize that minor trauma can cause significant skin injury.

statement: Decreased kidney function in elderly patients affects medication metabolism and elimination.
rationale: Kidney function declines with age affecting how medications are processed and eliminated. This can lead to drug accumulation and toxicity. Many medication-related emergencies in elderly are due to impaired kidney function.

statement: Elderly patients often present with atypical symptoms for common emergencies.
rationale: Normal aging and multiple comorbidities alter disease presentation. Elderly may have MI without chest pain infection without fever or surgical abdomen without significant pain. Maintain high suspicion and do not rely on typical symptom patterns.

statement: Confusion in an elderly patient is normal and does not require medical evaluation.
rationale: While dementia is common acute confusion or change from baseline mental status is not normal aging and requires evaluation. Acute confusion can indicate infection hypoglycemia medication effects stroke or other serious conditions.

statement: Baseline vital signs in elderly patients may differ from standard adult normal values.
rationale: Many elderly patients have baseline hypertension and may have normal lower heart rates due to medications. What appears normal may actually be abnormal for that individual. Ask about and compare to patient's baseline when possible.

statement: Polypharmacy increases the risk of drug interactions and adverse effects in elderly patients.
rationale: Many elderly patients take multiple medications for various conditions. Each additional medication increases risk of interactions and side effects. Document all medications thoroughly as drug effects may contribute to the emergency.

statement: Falls are a leading cause of injury and death in elderly patients.
rationale: Falls cause significant morbidity and mortality in elderly including hip fractures head injuries and death. Reduced bone density impaired balance medications and environmental hazards contribute. All elderly fall patients should be evaluated.

statement: Elderly patients on anticoagulants are at increased risk for serious bleeding even from minor trauma.
rationale: Blood thinners like warfarin and newer anticoagulants increase bleeding risk. Minor head trauma can cause life-threatening intracranial hemorrhage. Minor falls can cause significant internal bleeding. These patients require careful evaluation.

statement: Hip fractures in elderly patients can cause significant blood loss and shock.
rationale: Hip fractures are common after falls in elderly. Significant blood can be lost into the thigh and pelvis. Combined with decreased cardiovascular reserve elderly hip fracture patients are at risk for shock. Monitor closely.

statement: Elder abuse can include physical abuse neglect financial exploitation and emotional abuse.
rationale: Elder abuse takes many forms including physical abuse neglect of basic needs financial exploitation and emotional or psychological abuse. Be alert for signs including unexplained injuries poor hygiene and fearful behavior.

statement: Speaking loudly and slowly is always appropriate when communicating with elderly patients.
rationale: Do not assume all elderly patients have hearing loss. Assess hearing and adjust communication accordingly. Speaking loudly to a patient with normal hearing is disrespectful. Many elderly hear well and communicate normally.

statement: Elderly patients should be treated with respect and involved in decisions about their care.
rationale: Elderly patients deserve dignity and respect. Include them in conversations about their care rather than speaking only to family members. Explain procedures and respect their autonomy to make informed decisions including refusing care.

statement: During pregnancy blood volume increases by approximately 30-50 percent.
rationale: Pregnancy causes significant cardiovascular changes including 30-50% increase in blood volume increased heart rate and increased cardiac output. This provides reserve for blood loss during delivery but also means significant bleeding can occur before shock signs appear.

statement: The enlarged uterus can compress the inferior vena cava when a pregnant woman lies supine.
rationale: In late pregnancy the gravid uterus can compress the IVC causing supine hypotensive syndrome with decreased venous return hypotension and syncope. Position pregnant patients on their left side or with right hip elevated.

statement: Pregnant women have delayed gastric emptying increasing aspiration risk.
rationale: Pregnancy hormones slow gastric emptying and the enlarged uterus increases intra-abdominal pressure. This increases reflux and aspiration risk especially with altered mental status or during resuscitation. Suction equipment should be ready.

statement: Vaginal bleeding in pregnancy is always a medical emergency requiring evaluation.
rationale: Bleeding during pregnancy can indicate serious conditions including ectopic pregnancy miscarriage placenta previa and placental abruption. Any vaginal bleeding in pregnancy requires evaluation to determine the cause and ensure safety of mother and fetus.

statement: Ectopic pregnancy occurs when a fertilized egg implants outside the uterus usually in a fallopian tube.
rationale: Ectopic pregnancy most commonly occurs in the fallopian tube. As the pregnancy grows it can rupture causing life-threatening internal hemorrhage. Signs include abdominal pain vaginal bleeding and signs of shock in a woman of childbearing age.

statement: Placenta previa causes painless vaginal bleeding when the placenta covers the cervical opening.
rationale: In placenta previa the placenta implants over or near the cervix. As the cervix dilates late in pregnancy placental vessels tear causing painless bright red bleeding. This is an emergency requiring cesarean delivery.

statement: Placental abruption causes painful vaginal bleeding when the placenta separates from the uterine wall.
rationale: Abruption is premature placental separation causing painful bleeding rigid tender abdomen and fetal distress. Bleeding may be concealed behind the placenta. Trauma hypertension and cocaine use are risk factors. Both mother and fetus are at risk.

statement: Preeclampsia is characterized by hypertension and protein in urine developing after 20 weeks of pregnancy.
rationale: Preeclampsia is a pregnancy-specific condition with high blood pressure and end-organ effects. Signs include severe headache visual changes upper abdominal pain and edema. Eclampsia adds seizures. Both threaten mother and fetus.

statement: Seizures during pregnancy eclampsia are life-threatening to both mother and fetus.
rationale: Eclampsia is seizures occurring in the context of preeclampsia. It threatens both maternal and fetal life through hypoxia placental abruption and other complications. Transport immediately. Magnesium sulfate is the hospital treatment.

statement: Regular contractions that are increasing in frequency and intensity indicate labor is progressing.
rationale: True labor contractions become progressively more frequent longer and stronger over time. They do not decrease with activity or position changes. Contractions every 2-3 minutes with urge to push indicate delivery may be imminent.

statement: The urge to push or the feeling of needing to have a bowel movement indicates the baby is descending.
rationale: As the baby descends through the birth canal it puts pressure on the rectum creating sensation similar to needing to defecate. This urge to push indicates delivery is near. Check for crowning if the patient reports this sensation.
